Hands-On Lab · App Modernization

I Modernized a Legacy Java App with AWS Transform—Here’s What Happened

Java 8 → Java 21. 18 files. 528 lines changed. 422 hours saved. One agentic CLI command. Here’s the full story.

📅 March 26, 2026 ⏱ 12 min read ☁️ AWS · Java · Modernization · Agentic AI
☰ In this article
  1. What is AWS Transform?
  2. Key Features & Concepts
  3. Setting Up AWS Transform
  4. The App: Spring Boot Petclinic on Java 8
  5. Creating a Workspace & Starting a Job
  6. Running the Transformation (and the error I hit first)
  7. The Results: What the Agent Actually Did
  8. The App Runs on Java 21
  9. What’s Next: Pentesting with AWS Security Agent
  10. Key Takeaways

Legacy Java apps don’t age gracefully. Java 8 reached end of open-source support in 2019, Spring Boot 2.x followed in November 2023, and yet countless production codebases are still running on them—because upgrading manually is painful, risky, and frankly boring. AWS Transform promises to automate exactly this through agentic AI. I decided to put it to the test on a real Spring Boot petclinic app and document everything: setup, the first error I hit, what the agent changed, and whether the app actually ran afterward.

Spoiler: it worked. The agent made 528 line changes across 18 files, the dashboard showed 100% validation rate, and the petclinic came up clean on Java 21. Here’s the full story.

What is AWS Transform?

AWS Transform is AWS’s platform for enterprise IT modernization powered by agentic AI. It handles multiple transformation types: mainframe refactoring (formerly AWS Blu Insights), VMware migration, Windows modernization, and—the one we care about—Custom workloads, which covers Java, Python, and Node.js runtime upgrades. The pitch on their landing page says it all:

It’s not a static code migration tool. The agent actually reasons about your codebase—reading dependency trees, understanding what each library does, and making coordinated changes across multiple files. The scale of usage visible on their dashboard reflects how much enterprises have leaned on it:

1.35M+ Hours of manual effort saved
2.66B+ Lines of code analyzed
80x Faster than manual migration

Key Features & Concepts

Before diving into the walkthrough, it helps to understand the building blocks. AWS Transform has a few core concepts that work together:

🌐 Workspaces

Containers where you create jobs, store artifacts, and collaborate. Think of them as project folders — but with an AI agent as the project manager.

🤖 Agentic Job Plans

Structured multi-step workflows. The agent guides you through selecting a transformation definition, then monitors progress as the CLI executes against your repo.

🔗 Connectors

Outgoing and incoming connectors for cross-account resource access. Useful for multi-account enterprise setups; I didn't need any for this single-account demo.

🤝 Partner Agents

Third-party transformation agents from AWS partners. They get deep workspace access, so review the security documentation before enabling any.

📄 Transformation Definitions

AWS-managed recipes for specific upgrade types. AWS/java-version-upgrade handles the full stack: JDK bump, Jakarta EE migration, database drivers, Spring ecosystem.

💻 CLI (atx)

The AWS Transform CLI runs transformations locally against your codebase. Currently supports Custom workloads only — Java, Python, and Node.js. The actual code changes happen on your machine.

Setting Up AWS Transform

AWS Transform lives in the AWS console. You need to enable two things: the web application (the chat-based agentic UI) and the CLI (for actually executing transformations against local code).

Enable Capabilities

From the landing page, click Get Started. The capabilities page shows two sections — CLI for Custom workloads, and Web application for the full scope including mainframe and VMware:

Choose Your Identity Provider

Enabling the web application requires an identity provider. You pick between AWS Identity Center or an existing IdP (Microsoft Entra or Okta). This is a one-time irreversible decision:

🚨 One-time decision: You cannot change the identity provider type after enabling. Pick the right one for your org before clicking Enable.

Setup Complete

After enabling, the success page confirms the web application is live and a service profile has been created:

Install the CLI

curl -fsSL https://desktop-release.transform.us-east-1.api.aws/install.sh | bash

✅ Requirements: Node 20+ must be installed. The CLI does not run natively on Windows (use WSL). Configure AWS credentials with the required IAM policies before running any atx commands.

The App: Spring Boot Petclinic on Java 8

I needed a realistic legacy Java app—not a toy project, but something with real dependencies that would exercise the agent’s full capability. The Spring Boot petclinic is perfect: it’s a well-known demo app with Spring Boot 2.7.3, pinned to Java 1.8, and carrying a full stack of dependencies that all need updating.

The key configuration lines:

<artifactId>spring-petclinic</artifactId>
<version>2.7.3</version>

<parent>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
  <version>2.7.3</version>
</parent>

<java.version>1.8</java.version>

This is a textbook modernization target. Upgrading manually means: bumping Java version, migrating javax.* to jakarta.* across every import, updating database drivers, updating Spring Boot to a 3.x line, resolving cascading dependency conflicts, and validating that the app still builds and runs. AWS Transform is supposed to do all of that autonomously.

Creating a Workspace & Starting a Job

Create a Workspace & Job

In the AWS Transform console, create a workspace, then inside it create a new CustomTransformation job. You’re dropped into the agentic chat interface with a two-step job plan in the left sidebar:

Chat with the Agent

I typed exactly what I wanted: “Upgrade the Java 8 Spring Boot application to Java 21.”

The agent confirmed the plan and, after I approved, generated the CLI command with the campaign ID pre-filled and opened the Monitor panel with step-by-step instructions:

Running the Transformation — And Hitting an Error First

I copied the command from the dashboard and ran it. First attempt: I forgot the --configuration flag.

❌ Error: Non-interactive mode requires --configuration (or -g) with additionalPlanContext populated — the target language version must be provided before the agent runs headlessly.

Three ways to pass the config, as shown in the error output:

# Inline flag (what I used)
--configuration "additionalPlanContext=The target Java version to upgrade to is Java 21"

# JSON file
--configuration "file://path/to/config.json"
# config.json: { "additionalPlanContext": "The target Java version to upgrade to is Java 21" }

# YAML file
# config.yaml:
# additionalPlanContext: The target Java version to upgrade to is Java 21

With the flag added, the second attempt ran cleanly:

The full working command:

atx custom def exec \
  --code-repository-path ~/petclinic-legacy-demo \
  --non-interactive \
  --trust-all-tools \
  --campaign ced189bc-bafb-4465-8d67-bda247aaf391 \
  --repo-name petclinic-legacy-demo \
  --add-repo \
  --configuration "additionalPlanContext=The target Java version to upgrade to is Java 21"

Terminal output:

Fetching campaign details for 'ced189bc-bafb-4465-8d67-bda247aaf391'...
Using transformation definition: AWS/java-version-upgrade
Adding 'petclinic-legacy-demo' repository to campaign...
Marking 'petclinic-legacy-demo' repository as in progress...
[You are using an AWS Managed transformation.]

: Thinking...

✅ --trust-all-tools skips per-tool confirmation prompts. Combined with --non-interactive, the agent runs fully autonomously from this point. The "Thinking..." cursor means it’s analyzing the codebase — reading pom.xml, scanning imports, mapping dependencies.

The Results: What the Agent Actually Did

This is where it gets interesting. The agent didn’t just bump a version number. It worked through the codebase in six structured, independently-committed steps—each one building on the last, each one passing its own build validation before continuing.

The Transformation Dashboard

528 Lines of code modified
18 Files changed
100% Validation rate

The Six-Step Commit History

The agent committed its work in six sequential steps, each one labeled and verified. Here’s the full commit log from the atx-result-staging branch on GitHub:

Breaking down exactly what each step did:

1
Step 1: Establish Build Baseline with Source Java Version
ATX Bot · 4 hours ago · ✅ Build status: Success · 441f15e
2
Step 2: Update Build Tools and Maven Compiler Plugin for Java 21 Compatibility
ATX Bot · 4 hours ago · ✅ Build status: Success · 732e042
3
Step 3: Jakarta EE Migration — Update Dependencies and Migrate All javax.* Imports to jakarta.* Atomically
ATX Bot · 4 hours ago · ✅ Build status: Success · f7a51fc
4
Step 4: Update Database Drivers (H2, MySQL, PostgreSQL)
ATX Bot · 3 hours ago · ✅ Build status: Success · 94df524
5
Step 5: Upgrade Spring Boot to 3.2.12 and Update Spring Ecosystem Dependencies
ATX Bot · 3 hours ago · ✅ Build status: Success · c534920
6
Step 6: Final Integration Verification and Cleanup
ATX Bot · 3 hours ago · ✅ Build status: Success · 1bbbde7

The final commit visible at the top (ad6a4bc, labeled simply "aws transform") is my own commit — adding the repo to the campaign before the agent started. Everything below that is pure ATX Bot.

🤖 What makes this remarkable: The agent didn’t bundle everything into one massive commit. It worked in isolation — each step validates that the build still passes before committing and moving to the next. If step 3 had broken the build, it would have stopped and reported back rather than proceeding to break steps 4 through 6.

What the Agent Actually Changed

Reading between the commit messages, here’s what the agent touched across 18 files and 528 lines:

💾 pom.xml

Java version property updated to 21. Spring Boot parent bumped from 2.7.3 to 3.2.12. Maven compiler plugin updated. All dependency versions reconciled.

🔄 javax → jakarta

Every javax.* import across all source files migrated to jakarta.* atomically in a single step. This alone touches dozens of lines across multiple classes.

🖤 Database Drivers

H2, MySQL, and PostgreSQL driver dependencies updated to versions compatible with Jakarta EE and Spring Boot 3.x. Configuration changes to match new driver APIs where needed.

🧩 Spring Ecosystem

Spring Boot starter dependencies, Spring Data, Spring MVC, and related ecosystem libraries all updated as a coherent set — not individually, but as a coordinated upgrade.

Estimated Time Saved: 422 Hours

The dashboard calculated 422 hours of manual effort saved, using a conservative baseline of 10 lines of code per developer per day. At that rate, 528 lines would take roughly 53 developer-days. Even if you think the actual rate would be faster for a senior Java developer, the cognitive load of tracking every javax→jakarta migration, every dependency version conflict, and every Spring Boot 3 breaking change is substantial. The agent handled it in one run.

The App Runs on Java 21

The proof that matters: does the app actually work after the transformation? I deployed the transformed code to an EC2 instance and hit it in a browser.

The app came up with no code changes from my side. Home page renders, navigation works (Home, Find Owners, Veterinarians, Error), Spring logo loads. The transformation was complete and functional out of the box.

🎉 It works. Java 8 Spring Boot 2.7.3 application, transformed to Java 21 Spring Boot 3.2.12, running in production on EC2—zero manual code edits required.

What’s Next — Part 2: Pentesting the Modernized App

The app is live on Java 21. But a modernized app isn’t necessarily a secure app. In Part 2, I’ll walk through how I pentested this exact petclinic deployment using the AWS Security Agent—running vulnerability scans, probing the attack surface, and seeing what AWS’s AI-powered security tooling finds on a freshly modernized Spring Boot application.

🔐 Coming in Part 2: Pentesting the modernized petclinic with AWS Security Agent — what vulnerabilities surface, how the agent reports them, and what a real security posture looks like post-transformation.

Key Takeaways

⚡ Setup is fast

From zero to a running transformation took under 30 minutes including IAM Identity Center setup and CLI installation.

🤖 The agent is genuinely capable

It correctly identified the right transformation definition without prompting, worked through 6 structured steps, validated each build, and produced a working application.

📝 Don't forget --configuration

In non-interactive mode, --configuration "additionalPlanContext=..." is required. The error message is clear, but easy to miss when copying the base command from the dashboard.

🔎 Incremental commits are key

Six separate commits, each with a passing build, means you can review exactly what changed at each stage—and roll back to a specific step if something looks wrong.

🌟 It goes well beyond pom.xml

The javax→jakarta migration alone touched multiple source files. The agent understood the semantic meaning of each change, not just find-and-replace.

⏱ 422 hours is a real number

Even at a faster LoC/day rate, the coordination cost of a Java 8→21 migration is substantial. The agent handles it in one command.

AWS Transform Java 8 to 21 Spring Boot 3 Jakarta EE App Modernization Agentic AI atx CLI Legacy Java

Written March 26, 2026 · AWS Transform is Generally Available · CLI for Custom workloads available in us-east-1
